title: Good Deflation, Bad Deflation: Dividend vs. Penance
body_html:
The essay’s most useful distinction is between two completely different phenomena that get lazily bundled under the same word. Good deflation is what happens when entrepreneurs discover better ways to organize matter, labor, time, and knowledge. Costs fall because competence rises. The same inputs produce more output, or better output, and the benefits show up as lower prices. This is not a bug in capitalism; it is one of its clearest success conditions.

Bad deflation is something else entirely. It happens when a credit boom built on weak assumptions reverses, liquidity disappears, balance sheets crack, and everybody runs for cash at once. In that world, falling prices are symptoms of forced liquidation, not trophies of progress. The confusion starts when economists observe the pain of a credit unwind and decide that price declines themselves must be the disease, rather than the hangover from leverage and monetary distortion.

Once you separate the two, a lot of policy rhetoric collapses. Printing money does not cure good deflation because good deflation does not need curing. It simply muddies the signals produced by innovation and capital discipline. And printing money does not really cure bad deflation either; it socializes the costs of prior errors, props up weak structures, and often sets the stage for the next round of fragility.

That is why the essay’s framing is so powerful. Good deflation is the dividend of competence. Bad deflation is the penance of hubris. If you cannot distinguish them, you end up treating economic progress as a systemic threat and credit excess as something to be perpetually re-inflated.
